items.q: What does 'el argumento' mean when discussing a novel or film?
items.correct: The plot - the sequence of events in the work.
items.misconception: It means an argument or quarrel between the characters.
items.random_wrong: It means the cover design of the book.
items.why: 'Argumento' is a false friend: using it for 'quarrel' (una discusión, una pelea) muddles essay vocabulary that examiners expect to be precise.

items.q: Will retelling the story earn high marks in the essay?
items.correct: No - marks come from analysing how themes, characters and techniques create meaning, supported by selected evidence.
items.misconception: A detailed summary of the plot shows the examiner you know the work.
items.random_wrong: Essays are marked only on their length.
items.why: The assessment rewards critical response; narration without analysis stays in the lowest bands even when every plot detail is correct.

items.q: Is symbolism only relevant when studying poetry?
items.correct: No - films and novels use symbols and recurring motifs too, and directors add visual symbolism through images, colour and music.
items.misconception: Simbolismo belongs to poetry, so it is irrelevant to films.
items.random_wrong: Symbolism only exists in silent films.
items.why: Identifying symbolic images and motifs is a core analytical tool for the film and text questions, not a poetry-only technique.

items.q: In literary study, 'el argumento' means what?
items.why: It's The plot. “An argument between characters” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A strong essay paragraph about a text or film should do what?
items.correct: Make a point and support it with a specific scene or quotation
items.misconception: Retell the whole story in order
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Make a point and support it with a specific scene or quotation. “Retell the whole story in order” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is 'la banda sonora' of a film?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The soundtrack. “The opening credits” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: 'El desenlace' of a story refers to what?
items.why: It's The ending or resolution. “The list of chapters” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
